title: Control Valve Authority Range
summary: Valve authority should be 30-50% for piping systems with 2:1 branch-to-riser pressure drop ratios, with higher Δt coils requiring less authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided.
evidence_quote: Typically, authorities in the range of 30 to 50% (for piping systems with 2:1 branch-to-riser pressure drop ratios) are adequate for HVAC coil applications, with higher Δt coils requiring less valve authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided

title: Valve Authority for Variable-Speed Pumping
summary: In variable-speed pumping systems with differential pressure control, valve authority of 50 to 100% provides good results, implying a pressure drop on the valve equal to that of the coil.
evidence_quote: In the previous coils, a 6.7 K Δt was used, and valve authority of 50 to 100% provides good results, implying a pressure drop on the valve equal to that of the coil.

title: Coil Rating Parameters per AHRI Standard 410
summary: Dry-surface and dehumidifying coils are rated within: entering air dry-bulb 18-38°C, wet-bulb 15-30°C, air face velocity 1-4 m/s, evaporator saturation -1 to 13°C, chilled water 1.5-18°C, water velocity 0.3-2.4 m/s.
evidence_quote:
Entering air dry-bulb temperature: 18 to 38°C
Entering air wet-bulb temperature: 15 to 30°C
Air face velocity: 1 to 4 m/s
Evaporator refrigerant saturation temperature: –1 to 13°C
Entering chilled-water temperature: 1.5 to 18°C
Water velocity: 0.3 to 2.4 m/s

title: Coil Air Face Velocity for Dehumidifying Coils
summary: Maximum coil air face velocity should be limited to prevent water carryover; dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s.
evidence_quote: The maximum coil air face velocity should be limited to a value that prevents water carryover into the air ductwork. Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity.

title: Standard Rating Point for Unitary Equipment
summary: Standard rating point: 54 L/s per kW of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s per kW. For water coils, circuitry operates at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL/s per kW).
evidence_quote: a standard rating point has been 54 L/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s per kilowatt for unitary equipment. ... For water coils, circuitry would operate at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect).

title: Standard Rating Entering Air Conditions
summary: Standard ratings at 26.7°C db and 19.5°C wb are representative of entering air conditions encountered in many comfort operations.
evidence_quote: The standard ratings at 26.7°C db and 19.5°C wb are representative of the entering air conditions encountered in many comfort operations.

title: Coil Airflow Resistance Difference
summary: A completely dry coil offers approximately one-third less resistance to airflow than a dehumidifying coil removing both sensible and latent heat.
evidence_quote: A completely dry coil, removing only sensible heat, offers approximately one-third less resistance to airflow than a dehumidifying coil removing both sensible and latent heat.

title: Hydrophilic Coating Pressure Drop Reduction
summary: Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed to a new untreated coil.
evidence_quote: Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed a new untreated coil.

title: Low-Temperature Heating System Supply Temperatures
summary: Low-temperature heating systems are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K.
evidence_quote: They are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K.

title: Historical Hot-Water Heating Design
summary: Historically, hot-water heating systems were designed for a 93°C supply water temperature and an 11 K temperature drop.
evidence_quote: In the United States, hot-water heating systems were historically designed for a 93°C supply water temperature and an 11 K temperature drop.

title: Stratified System Supply Velocity
summary: Stratified room air distribution systems typically use supply outlets with very low discharge velocities (e.g., 0.2 to 0.36 m/s based on total face area).
evidence_quote: Stratified room air distribution systems, typically called thermal displacement ventilation, generally rely on supply outlets with very low discharge velocities (e.g., 0.2 to 0.36 m/s based on total face area)

title: Air Diffusion Performance Index (ADPI) Tool
summary: The air diffusion performance index (ADPI) is a useful tool in predicting outlet performance for fully mixed systems.
evidence_quote: The air diffusion performance index (ADPI) is a useful tool in predicting outlet performance for fully mixed systems

title: Coil Freezing Threshold
summary: Freezing occurs when a dehumidification coil’s surface temperature falls below 0°C. It does not occur with standard coils unless refrigerant evaporating temperature at coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated.
evidence_quote: Freezing occurs when a dehumidification coil’s surface temperature falls below 0°C. Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated

title: Ethylene Glycol Solution Rating Parameters
summary: For ethylene glycol solution: water velocity 0.3 to 1.8 m/s, entering dry-bulb –18 to 32°C, entering wet-bulb 15 to 27°C, 10 to 60% aqueous glycol concentration by mass.
evidence_quote: For ethylene glycol solution: 0.3 to 1.8 m/s, –18 to 32°C entering dry-bulb temperature, 15 to 27°C entering wet-bulb temperature, 10 to 60% aqueous glycol concentration by mass

title: Airflow Ratings Standard Air Density
summary: Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 1.20 kg/m³ at 20°C and a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa.
evidence_quote: Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 1.20 kg/m³ at 20°C and a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa.
